گفتار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گُفْتَار (guftār), from گفتن (“to say, tell”). By surface analysis, گُفْت (guft) + َار (ār).

ex.u: باغوں میں گُل کِھلے ہَیں۔
ex.r: bāġõ mẽ gul khile ha͠i.
ex.e: Flowers have bloomed in the gardens.

ex.u: گُلِ گُلْزار
ex.r: gul-i gulzār
ex.e: Rose from the flowerbed

گلا.e: From Sanskrit गल (gala). Ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *gʷel- (“throat”). Cognate with Latin gula (“throat”), English gullet, and Persian گلو (galu, “throat”).

ex.u: گلابی آنکھیں
ex.r: gulābī ā̃khẽ
ex.e: rosy eyes

ex.u: گلابی چدر
ex.r: gulābī caddar
ex.e: pink shawl/veil

گلابی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گُلَابِی (gulābī). By surface analysis, گُلاب (gulāb, “rosewater, rose”) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: rose garden, flower garden, garden, flower-bed

ex.u:
سارے جہاں سے اچھا ہندوستاں ہمارا
ہم بلبلیں ہیں اس کی، یہ گلستاں ہمارا
sāre jahā̃ se acchā, hindostā̃ hamārā.
ex.r: ham bulbulẽ ha͠i iskī, yah gulistā̃ hamārā.
ex.e:
Better than the whole world, is our Hindustan;
We are its nightingales, and it is our flower-garden.

گلستان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گُلِسْتَان (gulistān). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi گلستان (glstan /⁠gulistān⁠/). By surface analysis, گُل (gul, “flower”) + ـِسْتَان (-istān).

ex.u: جنگجو کا گُلْشَن ہے فردوس۔
ex.r: jangjū kā gulśan hai firdaus.
ex.e: the highest heaven is the warrior's garden.

senses.g: a place of joy and happiness
like a flower, rosy, delicate, beautiful
of something in likeness of a flower, rosy, beautiful
Gilgit (the capital city of Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan)
Gilgit-Baltistan (an administrative territory of Pakistan, part of Pakistani-Administered Kashmir, as well as the wider Kashmir region)

گلگت بلتستان.e: From گلگت (“Gilgit”) + بلتستان (“Baltistan”), the latter borrowed from Classical Persian بلتستان (baltistân), equivalent to بلتی (“Balti people”) + ـستان (“-land”).

گلو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گلو (gulō). Cognate with Sanskrit गल (gala, “throat, neck”). Doublet of گلا (galā).

گلی.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀕𑀮𑀻 (*galī). Cognate with Punjabi ਗਲੀ (galī) / گَلی (galī), Gujarati ગલી (galī), Sindhi ڳَليِ (g̠alyi) / ॻली, Marathi गल्ली (gallī), Bengali গলি (goli).

senses.g: Galilee (a mountainous geographic region in northern Israel)
a garment made out of wool

گلیم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گلیم (gilīm), from Ancient Greek κάλυμμα (kálumma, “cover, top, covering”) derived from καλύπτω (kalúptō, “to cover”).

گمانا.e: From گم (gum, “lost, missing”) + آ (ā, causative augment) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix).

ex.u: اُس کے کوئی اَچّھے گُن ہَیں ہی نَہِیں۔
ex.r: us ke koī acche gun ha͠i hī nahī̃.
ex.e: He has no good qualities at all.

گنا.e: Borrowed from Punjabi ਗੰਨਾ (gannā) / گَنّا (gannā). Doublet of گَان٘ڈَا (gā̃ḍā).

گناہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گناه (gunāh). Doublet of جُناح (junāh).

گنبذ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گنبذ (gunbaz, gumbaz, “dome, arch, cupola”).

گند.e: Inherited from Sanskrit गन्ध (gandha, “smell, stink”). Cognate with Persian گنده (gande, “stenchy”).

گندم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گندم (gandom). Doublet of گیہُوں (gehū̃).

ex.u:
جب کہ ایسا ہو گندمی لونڈا
تب گنہگار کیوں نہ ہو آدم
jab ki aisā ho gandumī lauṇḍā
ex.r: tab gunahgār kyõ na ho ādam
ex.e:
when there is such a wheat-complexioned boy
why then should not Adam be a sinner?

گندمی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گندمی (gandumī), from گندم (gandum, “wheat”).

گندہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گَنْدَه (ganda). First attested in c. 1679 as Middle Hindi گندہ (gndh /⁠ganda⁠/).

senses.g: Ganges (a major river in the states of Uttarakhand, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar and West Bengal, India; a major river in Rajshahi, Dhaka, Chittagong and Barisal Divisions, Bangladesh)
the holy water of the Ganges

گنی.e: Borrowed from English guinea and Guinea.
Borrowed from English guinea and Guinea.

senses.g: a male given name, Ganesh, from Sanskrit, of Hindu usage
Gwadar (a port city in Balochistan, Pakistan)

گوادر.e: Borrowed from Baluchi گوادر (gwādar), a compound of گوات (gwát, “wind, air”) + در (dar, “door, gateway”).

گوبھی.e: Borrowed from Portuguese couve (“cabbage”), from Latin caulis (“stalk, stem”). Cognate with Punjabi گوبھی (gobhī), Marathi कोबी (kobī), Bengali কপি (kopi), Assamese কবি (kobi), Manipuri ꯀꯣꯕꯤ (kobi). Some believe a derivative from Sanskrit गोजीह्विका (gojīhvikā), but it is unlikely.

senses.g: small round stone — piece, gamepiece (particularly in carom)
small round stone — game played using small round stones

گوٹی.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀕𑁄𑀝𑁆𑀝 (*goṭṭa, “something round”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-).

گوجرانوالہ.e: From Punjabi گوجرانوالہ (gujranvālā), named after the Gujjars.

senses.g: Gojra (a city in Toba Tek Singh district, Punjab, Pakistan)

گودام.e: Borrowed from Bengali গুদাম (gudam) or its etymon Portuguese gudão, from Malay gudang, from Tamil கிடங்கு (kiṭaṅku, “store room”).

گور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گور (gōr), from Middle Persian gpl, gwbl (gabr, “hollow, cavity, womb”).
Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit गौरि (gauri), variant of गौरी (gaurī).

گورا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀕𑁄𑀭 (gora), from Sanskrit गौर (gaurá).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀕𑁄𑀭 (gora), from Sanskrit गौर (gaurá).

senses.g: Gurdaspur (a city and administrative headquarters of Gurdaspur District in the Indian state of Punjab)

ex.u: لیکن پنجابی کا جو لہجہ سب کے لیے معیار قرار پایا، جس کی گرامر و اصول سب کے لئے قابل قبول ہوئے وہ لاہور، امرتسر، گورداسپور، سیالکوٹ اور ان کے نواحی علاقوں کا ہے۔
ex.r: lekin panjābī kā jo lahja sab ke liye miʻyar qarār pāya, jis kī grāmar o aṣūl sab ke liy'e qābl-e-qabūl hū'e vo lāhaur, amritsar, gordāspūr, siyalkoṭ aur un ke nuvāḥī ʻilāqoṉ kā hai.
ex.e: However, the dialect of Punjabi which was set as the standard for all, and whose grammar and principles became acceptable to all, belongs to Lahore, Amritsar, Gurdaspur, Sialkot and their suburbs.

senses.g: Gurdaspur District (a district of Punjab, India)

ex.u: میاں فضل حسین کا تعلق بھٹی راجپوت سے تھا اور ان کا خاندان گو بٹالہ ضلع گورداسپور کا متوطن تھا
ex.r: miyaṉ faẓl ḥusain kā taʻluq bhaṭṭī rājpūt se thā āur un kā xāndān go baṭāla ẓilaʻ gordāspūr kā mutavat̤t̤in thā
ex.e: Mian Fazl Hussain belonged to Bhatti Rajput and his family were from Batala, district Gurdaspur

گورداسپور.e: From گورداس (gordās) + ـپُور (-pūr, “town; city; village”). Named after Mahant Guriya das ji.

گورستان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گورِسْتَان (gōristān). By surface analysis, گور (gor) + ـسْتَان (-stān).

گورکھا.e: Borrowed from Nepali गोर्खा (gorkhā, “(inhabitant of) Gorkha District”) named for गोरख (gorakh, “Gorakhnath”), from Sanskrit गोरक्ष (gorakṣa, “cowherd”).

گورمنٹ.e: Semi-learned borrowing from English government, initially a transliteration of English government.

گورو.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit गौरव (gaurava, “cumbrousness, importance”); see गौर (gaura, “shining, brilliant”) for more.

senses.g: a male given name, Gaurav, from Sanskrit

گورو.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit गौरव (gaurava, “cumbrousness, importance”); see गौर (gaura, “shining, brilliant”) for more.

ex.u: ہم نے گوزن کا شکار کیا اور اس کے سینگ کاٹ لیے۔
ex.r: ham ne gavazn kā śikār kiyā aur us ke sīng kāṭ liye
ex.e: We hunted a stag and cut off its antlers.

گوزن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گوزن (gawazn, “deer; stag”)

گوشت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گوشت (gōšt). Compare Assamese গোছ (güs), Bengali গোশত (gōśot), Punjabi گوشت / ਗੋਸ਼ਤ (gośat) and Gujarati ગોશ્ત (gośt).

گوگرد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گوگرد (gōgird, gūgird).

گولکنڈہ.e: From Telugu గొల్లకొండ (gollakoṇḍa), from గొల్ల (golla, “of or pertaining to shepherds”) + కొండ (koṇḍa, “hill”).

گولی.e: Inherited from Sanskrit गोलिका (golikā). Cognate with Persian گلوله (golule).

senses.g: mute (of a person, unable to speak)

گہرا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀕𑀳𑀻𑀭 (gahīra), from Sanskrit गभीर (gabhīrá).

senses.g: a mortgage (ie. a deposit for a guarantee)

گہنا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi گہنا (ghna /⁠gahnā⁠/) (first attested in c. 1697),, from Prakrit 𑀕𑀳𑀦 (gahana) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit ग्र॑हण (gràhaṇa, “holding, putting on”). Compare later Sanskrit गहना (gahanā, “ornament”), a borrowing from Middle Indo-Aryan.

senses.g: to do something with effort or loudly

گہنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀕𑀳𑀇 (gahaï), from Sanskrit *ग्रहति (*grahati, class 1 verb), from ग्रह् (grah, “to grab”), from Proto-Indo-European *gʰrebh₂-. Compare verbs Sanskrit गृ॒भा॒यति॑ (gṛbhāyáti), गृ॒ह्णाति॑ (gṛhṇā́ti).

senses.g: form of the suffix ـی (-ī) for words that end in the short vowel ـہ (-a)

ـگی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ـگِی (-gī). First attested as Middle Hindi گی (-gy /⁠-gī⁠/).

senses.g: masculine singular perfect adjectival of جانا (jānā): went, left

ex.u: وہ گھر گیا۔
ex.r: vo ghar gayā
ex.e: He went home.

گیا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀕𑀅 (gaa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit गत (gatá, “gone”).

گیارہ.e: Inherited from Old Hindi ग्यारह (gyāraha), a metathesis of इगारह (igāraha), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀏𑀕𑀸𑀭𑀲 (egārasa), from Sanskrit एकादश (ékādaśa).

گیت.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit गीत (gīta), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *giHtás, Proto-Indo-European *g⁽ʷ⁾iH-tós, from *geH- (“to sing, cry”).

گیدڑ.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀕𑀺𑀤𑁆𑀤 (*gidda, “jackal”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀟- (-ḍa-). Cognate with Bengali গিধড় (gidhoṛ), Punjabi ਗਿੱਦੜ (giddaṛ) / گِدَّڑ (giddaṛ) and Sindhi گِدَڙُ (gidaṛu).

گیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گیر (gīr, “a captor, taker, seizer”).

گیسن.e: Borrowed from English Giessen, itself from German Gießen.

گیسو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian گیسو (gēsō, gēsū).

گیلا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀕𑁆𑀭𑀺𑀮𑁆𑀮 (*grilla, “wet”). Compare Punjabi گِلّا (gillā) / ਗਿੱਲਾ (gillā).

گیند.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀕𑁂𑀦𑁆𑀤𑀼𑀕 (genduga), from Sanskrit कन्दुक (kanduka).

ex.u: گینڈے کی کھال ہے اس کی۔
ex.r: geṇḍe kī khāl hai us-kī.
ex.e: He has the hide of a rhinoceros (i.e., it is very difficult to upset him).

گیہوں.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀕𑁄𑀳𑀽𑀫 (gohūma), from Sanskrit गोधूम (godhū́ma, “wheat”). Doublet of گَنْدُم (gandum).

گھاس.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀖𑀸𑀲 (ghāsa), from Sanskrit घास (ghāsá, “food; pasture grass”).

senses.g: sunshine; heat or light of the sun

گھام.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀖𑀫𑁆𑀫 (ghamma), from Sanskrit घर्म (gharmá). Doublet of گَرْم (garm).

گھبرانا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀖𑀩𑁆𑀪𑀭 (*ghabbhara). Compare Marathi घाबरणे (ghābarṇe).

senses.g: to decrease, to dwindle, to decline

گھٹنا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi घटना (ghaṭanā), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀖𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀤𑀺 (ghaṭṭadi), from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀖𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀢𑀺 (*ghaṭṭati), perhaps derived from Sanskrit घट्ट् (ghaṭṭ, “to rub; to speak ill of”) in the "speak ill of" sense. Cognate with Maithili घटब (ghaṭab) / 𑒒𑒙𑒥 (ghaṭaba), Nepali घट्नु (ghaṭnu), Punjabi ਘਟਣਾ (ghaṭṇā) / گَھٹݨا (ghaṭṇā), Gojri گھٹنو (ghaṭno), Saraiki گَھٹَّݨ (ghaṭṭaṇ), Marwari घटणौ (ghaṭṇau) / 𑅘𑅞𑅢𑅒 (ghṭṇu), Sindhi گَهٽَڻُ (ghaṭaṇu) / घटणु / 𑊾𑋆𑋌𑋣 (ghaṭaṇu), Gujarati ઘટવું (ghaṭvũ), Marathi घटणे (ghaṭṇe), Assamese ঘটা (ghota), ঘাটা (ghata).

گھر.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀖𑀭 (ghara, a metathesized form of Sanskrit गृह (gṛha)), from Proto-Middle Indo-Aryan *𑀕𑀭𑁆𑀳 (*garha), from Sanskrit गृह (gṛha), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *gʰr̥dʰás, from Proto-Indo-European *gʰerdʰ-.

گھرانا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀖𑀭𑀸𑀬𑀬𑀡 (*gharāyayaṇa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-) from Sanskrit *घरायतन (*gharāyatana), from *घर (*ghara) + आयतन (āyátana), in which *घर (*ghara) is a reborrowed Prakrit form of गृह (gṛhá) (or गृध (gṛdha), which is from Proto-Indo-Iranian *gʰr̥dʰás, from Proto-Indo-European *gʰr̥dʰós, from *gʰerdʰ- (“to enclose”). Cognate with Punjabi ਘਰਾਨਾ (gharānā) / گھرانہ (ghrānh), Sindhi گھراڻو (ghrāṇo) / घराणो, Marathi घराणे (gharāṇe), Nepali घरान् (gharān).

گھرم.e: From Sanskrit घर्म (gharma). Doublet of گرم, a borrowing from Persian.

ex.u: گھریلو تشدد
ex.r: gharelū taśaddud
ex.e: domestic violence

گھمنڈ.e: Unknown, probably inherited from Sanskrit गरिमन् (gariman). Cognate with Punjabi ਘਮੰਡ (ghamaṇḍ) / گھَمَن٘ڈ (ghamaṉḍ).

گھن.e: Inherited from Prakrit घिणा (ghiṇā), from Sanskrit घृणा (ghṛṇā).

گھنا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi گهنا (ghna /⁠ghanā⁠/) (c. 1564), from Prakrit 𑀖𑀡 (ghaṇa, “dense”), from Sanskrit घ॒न (ghaná).

گھناؤنا.e: Derived from Sanskrit घृणा (ghṛṇā). Related to گِھن (ghin).

senses.g: inflection of گِھناؤنا (ghināonā): — direct masculine plural
inflection of گِھناؤنا (ghināonā): — oblique masculine singular/plural

گھنٹہ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀖𑀁𑀝 (ghaṃṭa), from Sanskrit घण्टा f (ghaṇṭā).

senses.g: inflection of گِھنَونا (ghinaunā): — direct masculine plural
inflection of گِھنَونا (ghinaunā): — oblique masculine singular/plural
inflection of گَھنا (ghanā): — direct masculine plural
inflection of گَھنا (ghanā): — oblique masculine singular/plural
Ghotki (a city in Ghotki district, Sindh, Pakistan)

گھوڑا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀖𑁄𑀟𑀬 (ghoḍaya), from Sanskrit घोटक (ghoṭaka), possibly from Proto-Dravidian *kut-ir-ay (“horse”), from *kuti (“to jump”), from *kut + *i. Cognate with Punjabi گھوڑا (ghoṛā), Sindhi گهوڙو (ghoṛo), Marathi घोडा (ghoḍā), Gujarati ઘોડો (ghoḍo), Bengali ঘোড়া (ghōṛa) and Odia ଘୋଡ଼ା (ghoṛā).

senses.g: to mix in (a substance into a liquid)

گھولنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀖𑁄𑀮𑁂𑀤𑀺 (gholedi), from Sanskrit घोलयति (gholayati), perhaps related to घूर्णति (ghū́rṇati).

گھونٹ.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa घुंट (ghuṃṭa), from Prakrit 𑀖𑀼𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀇 (ghuṭṭaï, “gulps”), of Dravidian origin. Cognate with Punjabi گُھٹّ (ghuṭṭ, “sip, gulp”), Gujarati ઘૂંટ (ghū̃ṭ), and Marathi घोट (ghoṭ).

senses.g: a blow with the fist; a punch

گھی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀖𑀺𑀤 (ghida), from Sanskrit घृत (ghṛtá). Compare Punjabi گِھیو (ghio) / ਘਿਉ (ghiu).

senses.g: The thirtieth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by گ and followed by م.

لاج.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi لاج (laj /⁠lāj⁠/) (c. 1564) / لج (lj /⁠lajj⁠/) (c. 1609) / لجہ (ljh /⁠lajja⁠/) (c. 1599) / لجا (lja /⁠lajjā⁠/), from Prakrit 𑀮𑀚𑁆𑀚𑀸 (lajjā), from Sanskrit लज्जा (lajjā).

لاجوردی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لاجوردی (lājwardī). By surface analysis, لاجْوَرْد (lājvard, “lapis lazuli”) + ـی (-ī).

لاحق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لاحق, from Arabic لَاحِق (lāḥiq).

senses.g: follower who joins the congregation from the beginning but loses one or more rak'ahs due to an excuse

لاحق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لاحق, from Arabic لَاحِق (lāḥiq).

senses.g: dear, darling (affectionate address to a child)

لاڈلہ.e: From Prakrit 𑀮𑀟𑁆𑀟𑀺𑀅 (laḍḍia, “caressing”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀮𑁆𑀮- (-lla-) + -𑀓- (-ka-), ultimately from Sanskrit लड् (laḍ, “to play”). By surface analysis, لاڈ (lāḍ, “affection”) + ـلا (-lā).
From Prakrit 𑀮𑀟𑁆𑀟𑀺𑀅 (laḍḍia, “caressing”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀮𑁆𑀮- (-lla-) + -𑀓- (-ka-), ultimately from Sanskrit लड् (laḍ, “to play”). By surface analysis, لاڈ (lāḍ, “affection”) + ـلا (-lā).

senses.g: Larkana (a city in Larkana district, Sindh, Pakistan)

لازم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لَازِم (lāzim), from Arabic لَازِم (lāzim).

لاشہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لاشه (lāša), an alternative form of لاش (lāš).

senses.g: Of or pertaining to Latin America

لاطینی.e: Possibly hyperforeignism of Arabic لَاتِينِيَّة (lātīniyya), from Latin Latīnus.
Possibly hyperforeignism of Arabic لَاتِينِيَّة (lātīniyya), from Latin Latīnus.

لاکھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀮𑀓𑁆𑀔 (lakkha), from Sanskrit लक्ष (lakṣa). Compare Bengali লাখ (lakh), Gujarati લાખ (lākh), Marathi लाख (lākh) and Nepali लाख (lākh).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀮𑀓𑁆𑀔 (lakkha), from Sanskrit लक्ष (lakṣa). Compare Bengali লাখ (lakh), Gujarati લાખ (lākh), Marathi लाख (lākh) and Nepali लाख (lākh).

senses.g: expense; cost, price (ie. the base cost of an item or service etc.)

لاگت.e: From لَگْنا (lagnā, “to be applied”) + ـَت (-at, nominal suffix).

لال بیگ.e: Corrupted from older لال بھیکھ (lāl bhekh, literally “red-clad”), from لال (lāl, “red”) + بھیکھ (bhekh, “religious attire”). Influenced by لال (lāl, “beloved; son”) and بیگ (beg), both common components in given names. The noun was likely originally used to offend members of the Chuhra caste, who traditionally adhered to the Balmiki sect of Hinduism and worked as sweepers in cockroach-abundant environments, and was probably reinforced by the comparison between the reddish colour of cockroaches and the red cloak of Valmiki.

Corrupted from older لال بھیکھ (lāl bhekh, literally “red-clad”), from لال (lāl, “red”) + بھیکھ (bhekh, “religious attire”). Influenced by لال (lāl, “beloved; son”) and بیگ (beg), both common components in given names. The noun was likely originally used to offend members of the Chuhra caste, who traditionally adhered to the Balmiki sect of Hinduism and worked as sweepers in cockroach-abundant environments, and was probably reinforced by the comparison between the reddish colour of cockroaches and the red cloak of Valmiki.

لال سلام.e: لَال (lāl) + سلام. An allusion to red being the color of communism.

لالٹین.e: Borrowed from Bengali লন্ঠন (lonṭhon), from English lantern. The Urdu spelling لالٹين (lālṭen) is attested in the آبِ حیات (Āb-e hayāt) in 1880.

ex.u:
لالۂ گل نے ہماری خاک پر ڈالا ہے شور
کیا قیامت ہے موؤں کو بھی ستاتی ہے بہار
lālā-e-gul ne hamārī xāk par ḍālā hai śor
ex.r: kyā qayāmat hai muõ ko bhī satātī hai bahār
ex.e:
The flowered tulip has alkalised our soil
What a disaster, spring torments even hairs

لالہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لاله (lāla), from Chagatai لاله (lāla).

senses.g: a title for a Hindu man

ex.u: شنکر لالہ
ex.r: śaṅkar lāla
ex.e: Mr. Shankar

senses.g: brother (a term of address for a male)

ex.u: ۱۹ بچے اور ۴ ٹیچرز، لالہ۔
ex.r: 19 bacce aur 4 ṭīcarz, lāla.
ex.e: 19 children and 4 teachers, brother.

لالہ.e: Compare Punjabi لَالَہ (lālah), Pashto لالا (lala), Persian لالا (lâlâ).

senses.g: Lollywood, a nickname for the Pakistani film industry, based in Lahore.

لالی وڈ.e: From Lahore and Hollywood; influenced by بالی وڈ (bālī oḍ, “Bollywood”), a nickname for the Indian film industry, based in Bombay.

senses.g: Lollywood, a nickname for the Pakistani film industry, based in Lahore

لالیوڈ.e: From Lahore and Hollywood; influenced by Bollywood, a nickname for the Indian film industry, based in Bombay.

لامتناہی.e: From لا (lā, “no, none”) + مُتَناہی (mutanāhī, “end, limit, finite”).

لانا.e: Two theories, or conflation of both: * A reduction from the light verb construction لے آنا (le ānā) with the same meaning, explaining the lack of an ergative marker for its subject as آنا (ānā) is intransitive. Compare Punjabi ਲਿਆਉਣਾ (liāuṇā) which more transparently is a lexicalized light verb construction, preserving the original vowels. Nepali ल्याउनु (lyāunu), Gujarati લાવવું (lāvvũ) may also reflect the same. This is the more likely etymology, examining the cognates. * Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀮𑀸𑀤𑀺 (lādi), from Sanskrit लाति (lā́ti), from the root ला (lā, “take”). The root ला (lā) is attested relatively early, in the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa. It is also present in Pāṇinī's Dhātupāṭha. A relation to लभ् (labh, “to gain”) may be justified as readaptation through Middle Indo-Aryan.

senses.g: Lahore (the second largest city of Pakistan and the provincial capital of Punjab; located on the Ravi river)

لاہور.e: Unknown or disputed. A legend claims to be inherited from Sanskrit लव (lava, “Lava”) + पुर (pura, “fortress; city”), literally “City of Lava”, from Ramayana, a Hindu epic. Compare Punjabi لَہَوْر (lahaur).

senses.g: upper lip, edge of the lip
edge; shore; margin (ie. of a river, coast etc.)

لب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لَب (lab). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi لب (lb /⁠lab⁠/).

لباس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لِباس, from Arabic لِبَاس (libās). Compare Punjabi ਲਿਬਾਸ (libās) / لِباس (libās), Gujarati લિબાસ (libās), લેબાસ (lebās).

senses.g: Lebanon (a country in West Asia in the Middle East)

لبھانا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀮𑀼𑀩𑁆𑀪𑀤𑀺 (lubbhadi), from Sanskrit लुभ्यति (lúbhyati).

senses.g: to stick, cling; to be joined

لپٹنا.e: From Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀮𑀺𑀧𑁆𑀧𑀤𑀺 (lippadi), from Sanskrit लिप्यते (lipyate).

لٹکنا.e: Urdu verb set لَٹَکْنَا (laṭaknā) لَٹْکانا (laṭkānā) Inherited from Prakrit *𑀮𑀝𑁆𑀝 (*laṭṭa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓𑁆𑀓- (-kka-). Cognate with Gujarati લટકવું (laṭakvũ), Marathi लटकणे (laṭakṇe).

senses.g: passive of لوٹنا (lūṭnā) — to be looted, to be stolen
passive of لوٹنا (lūṭnā) — to get plundered

لچک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لچک (lačak, lačik), with further etymology unknown.

ex.u: ہر لحظہ ہے مومن کی نئی شان، نئی آن
ex.r: har laḥz̤a hai momin kī nay'ī śān, nay'ī ān
ex.e: Every moment is a glory, a splendour for a believer

لحظہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لَحْظَه (lahza), borrowed from Arabic لَحْظَة (laḥẓa). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi لحظہ (lḥz̤h).

senses.g: inflection of لَحْظہ (“moment, instant”): — direct plural
inflection of لَحْظہ (“moment, instant”): — oblique/vocative singular

لحم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لَحْم (lahm), from Arabic لَحْم (laḥm).

لداخ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لداخ (ladâx), from Tibetan ལ་དྭགས (la dwags).

senses.g: Ludhiana (a city in Ludhiana district, Punjab, India)

لذت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لَذَّت (lazzat), borrowed from Arabic لَذَّة (laḏḏa), from لَذَّ (laḏḏa). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi لذت (lẕt /⁠lazzat⁠/).

لرزاں.e: Compare لرزنا (laraznā, “to tremble”), from Classical Persian لرزیدن (larzīdan).

ex.u: یہ لڑکا اتنا لمبا نہیں ہے جتنی اسکی بہن ہے۔
ex.r: yêh laṛkā itnā lambā nahī̃ hai jitnī uskī bahan hai.
ex.e: This boy is not as tall as his sister.

ex.u: میرا لڑکا کل شام پیدا ہوا تھا۔
ex.r: merā laṛkā kal śām paidā huā thā.
ex.e: My son was born yesterday evening.

لڑکا.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *लडिक्क (laḍikka). Related to the lexicographically-attested Sanskrit लाडिक (lāḍika, “boy, servant”), लट्व (laṭva, “dancing boy”) लट (laṭa, “fool”), and thus may ultimately come from Sanskrit लट् (laṭ, “to act like a child”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓𑁆𑀓- (-kka-). Cognate to Awadhi लरिका (larikā), Bhojpuri लईका (laīkā), Bengali লেড়কা (leṛoka).

لڑنا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit रटति (raṭati). Compare Gujarati લડવું (laḍvũ), Marathi लढणे (laḍhṇe).

senses.g: to roll over, roll away, roll down

لڑھکنا.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa लुढइ (luḍhaï) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓𑁆𑀓- (-kka-), from Prakrit 𑀮𑀼𑀠𑀇 (luḍhaï), from Sanskrit लुठति (luṭháti).

لسان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian لِسَان (lisān), from Arabic لِسَان (lisān).
